textInfo.title: Elevated Total Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: Cholesterin
textInfo.originalDescription: Cholesterin ': H 5.3 mmol/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your total cholesterol level is 5.3 mmol/L, which is above the recommended level of less than 5 mmol/L. High total cholesterol is a risk factor for cardiovascular disease. It is important to look at the breakdown of your cholesterol (HDL, LDL) for a complete picture.

textInfo.title: Normal Blood Pressure
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports consistently normal blood pressure.
textInfo.originalDescription: My blood pressure has always been perfect.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your blood pressure has always been perfect.

textInfo.title: Recurrent Ganglion Cyst on Right Wrist
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports recurrence of a ganglion cyst on the right wrist, which was previously surgically removed. The current cyst is asymptomatic regarding nerve compression.
textInfo.originalDescription: The right one developed a Ganglion Cyst around 2018. At the time the cyst was pressing on the nerve, which is why I had a surgery to remove it at Unispital Zurich. After some time, the Ganglion Cyst returned and is still there, however it was not pressing on the nerve anymore so I did not go for a second surgery.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: After having a ganglion cyst removed from your right wrist in 2018, it has returned. However, the new cyst is not pressing on the nerve, so you have not sought a second surgery.

textInfo.title: Occasional Wrist Inflammation
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports occasional inflammation of soft tissue at the wrist, involving both radial and ulnar aspects, at the junction with the forearm connective tissue.
textInfo.originalDescription: Occasionally (not constantly like with the elbows) I feel an inflammation of the soft tissue right where the connective tissue of the forearm connects to the wrist - both on the thumb side and the pinky side.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: You occasionally feel inflammation in the soft tissue where your forearm connects to your wrist, affecting both the thumb and pinky sides. This is not constant like your elbow inflammation.

textInfo.title: Low Albumin-Corrected Calcium
textInfo.medicalDescription: Albumin-korrigiertes Calcium
textInfo.originalDescription: Albumin-korrigiertes Calcium: L 2.09 mmol/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your albumin-corrected calcium level is 2.09 mmol/L, which is slightly below the normal range of 2.10-2.60 mmol/L. This measurement adjusts for the amount of calcium bound to albumin protein in the blood. A minor deviation like this is often not a cause for concern but may be re-checked.

textInfo.title: Elevated Triglycerides
textInfo.medicalDescription: Triglyceride
textInfo.originalDescription: Triglyceride': H 2.3 mmol/l (<2.0)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your triglyceride level is 2.3 mmol/l, which is above the recommended level of less than 2.0 mmol/l. Triglycerides are a type of fat found in your blood. High levels can increase the risk of heart disease.

textInfo.title: No Previous Heart Conditions
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient denies any previous cardiac conditions or problems.
textInfo.originalDescription: I do not have previous conditions or problems.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: You do not have any previously diagnosed heart conditions or problems.

textInfo.title: Elevated Liver Iron
textInfo.medicalDescription: Elevated liver R2* value of 111.2 s⁻¹, which is suggestive of hepatic iron overload.
textInfo.originalDescription: R2* ROI mean: 111.2s^-1
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The MRI scan measured a value called R2*, which is used to estimate the amount of iron in your liver. Your result of 111.2 s⁻¹ is higher than the typical range, which may indicate an excess of iron in the liver, a condition known as iron overload. Follow-up may be advised to investigate this further.

textInfo.title: Low Sodium Level
textInfo.medicalDescription: Natrium '
textInfo.originalDescription: Natrium ': L 133 mmol/l (136-145)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your sodium level is 133 mmol/l, which is slightly below the normal range of 136-145 mmol/l. Sodium is an important electrolyte that helps regulate fluid balance and nerve function. Mildly low levels may not cause symptoms, but it's worth monitoring.

textInfo.title: Low HDL Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: HDL-Cholesterin '
textInfo.originalDescription: HDL-Cholesterin ': L 0.99 mmol/l (>1.00)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your HDL (High-Density Lipoprotein) cholesterol level is 0.99 mmol/l, which is below the recommended level of over 1.00 mmol/l. HDL cholesterol is often called 'good' cholesterol because it helps remove other forms of cholesterol from your bloodstream. Lower levels can be associated with an increased risk of heart disease.

textInfo.title: Normal Spine Finding
textInfo.medicalDescription: Wirbelsäule: Regelrecht
textInfo.originalDescription: Wirbelsäule: Regelrecht
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of your spine shows no abnormalities. The vertebrae, discs, spinal cord, and surrounding structures were all evaluated and appear normal for your age. No signs of disc herniation, spinal stenosis (narrowing), or other significant issues were found.

textInfo.title: Slightly Low Sodium
textInfo.medicalDescription: Natrium
textInfo.originalDescription: Natrium ': L 133 mmol/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your sodium level is 133 mmol/L, which is slightly below the normal range of 136-145 mmol/L. This is a minor deviation and may not be clinically significant, but it's worth monitoring. Sodium is an important electrolyte that helps regulate fluid balance in the body.

textInfo.title: Elevated Non-HDL Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: Non-HDL-Cholesterin (berechnet)
textInfo.originalDescription: Non-HDL-Cholesterin (berechnet): H 4.3 mmol/l (<3.9)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your Non-HDL cholesterol level is 4.3 mmol/l, which is above the recommended level of less than 3.9 mmol/l. Non-HDL cholesterol includes all the 'bad' types of cholesterol and is considered a good indicator of cardiovascular risk.

textInfo.title: Small 10 mm Liver Lesion, Likely Benign
textInfo.medicalDescription: T2 hyperintense, rundliche Läsion (10 mm) an der Segmentgrenze VII/VIII. DD Zyste, Hämangiom.
textInfo.originalDescription: T2 hyperintense, rundliche Läsion (10 mm) an der Segmentgrenze VII/VIII.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: A second small, round lesion, measuring 10 mm, was identified on your MRI. It is located at the border of liver segments VII and VIII. Similar to the other finding, this lesion is 'T2 hyperintense,' indicating it is likely filled with fluid. The radiologist's differential diagnosis includes a simple cyst (a benign, fluid-filled sac) or a hemangioma (a non-cancerous tangle of blood vessels). Both possibilities are common and typically do not cause problems.

textInfo.title: Low Folic Acid (Folate)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Folsäure Serum '
textInfo.originalDescription: Folsäure Serum ': L 11.2 nmol/l (>12.2)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your serum folic acid (folate) level is 11.2 nmol/l, which is below the recommended level of above 12.2 nmol/l. Folic acid is a B-vitamin that is important for creating new cells. Low levels can lead to certain types of anemia.

textInfo.title: Altered Spleen Tissue Signal
textInfo.medicalDescription: Inhomogen T2 hypointenses Milzparenchym. Normalgrosse, signalalterierte Milz, DD Sarkoidose.
textInfo.originalDescription: Inhomogen T2 hypointenses Milzparenchym.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The MRI scan showed that the tissue of your spleen, which is of normal size, has an uneven or non-uniform signal. This is described as 'inhomogen T2 hypointenses Milzparenchym,' meaning parts of the spleen tissue appeared darker than expected on certain MRI sequences. The radiologist notes this altered signal appearance and suggests that sarcoidosis could be a possible, though unconfirmed, explanation. Sarcoidosis is an inflammatory condition that can affect various organs, including the spleen.

textInfo.title: Family History: Father's MTHFR Gene Mutation
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient's father has the MTHFR gene mutation, which impairs folate metabolism.
textInfo.originalDescription: Father: Has the MTHFR gene mutation and cannot process folate.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Father

textInfo.title: Not Tested for MTHFR Mutation
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient has not been tested for the MTHFR gene mutation.
textInfo.originalDescription: My father has the MTHFR gene mutation and cannot process folate. I have not been tested for this.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Although your father has the MTHFR gene mutation, you have not been tested for it yourself.

textInfo.title: Intermediate Cardiovascular Risk (hs-CRP)
textInfo.medicalDescription: CRP sensitiv '
textInfo.originalDescription: CRP sensitiv ': 2.4 mg/l (mittleres KHK Risiko: 1.0 - 3.0 mg/l)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your high-sensitivity C-Reactive Protein (hs-CRP) level is 2.4 mg/l. This value falls into the range for average risk (1.0 - 3.0 mg/l) of future cardiovascular events. hs-CRP is a marker of inflammation in the body.

textInfo.title: Past Surgery: Sinus/Fistula on Buttock
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient underwent surgery for a pilonidal sinus/fistula in 2011.
textInfo.originalDescription: Sinus/Fistula surgery on my butt in 2011.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: You had surgery on your buttock for a sinus or fistula in 2011.

textInfo.title: Sternum Cracking
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports sternal crepitus (cracking) during stretching movements.
textInfo.originalDescription: Finally, during stretching my sternum cracks sometimes.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your sternum (breastbone) sometimes cracks when you stretch.

textInfo.title: High Resting Heart Rate
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports a resting heart rate of approximately 70-90 bpm, which they consider somewhat high for their level of physical fitness (competitive sports, able to run 10km).
textInfo.originalDescription: My heart rate has always been somewhat high during rest (~70-90bpm) even when I have been practicing sports competitively and could run 10km without issues.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your resting heart rate has always been somewhat high, around 70-90 beats per minute, even when you were practicing sports competitively and could run 10km without issues.

textInfo.title: Low Left Psoas Major Muscle Fat Infiltration
textInfo.medicalDescription: The intramuscular fat (IMF) of the left psoas major muscle is 4.6%, which is below the normal range of 6% to 9%.
textInfo.originalDescription: {"biomarker_id":"0208013","biomarker_name":"IMF_psoas_major left","valueType":"NUMERICAL","value":4.6,"unit":"%","ReferenceMean":7.5,"Reference SD":1.2,"minNormalValue":6,"maxNormalValue":9,"Within 1.28*SD Range":false}
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The amount of fat within your left psoas major muscle, a key core muscle, is lower than average for your demographic. While lower fat infiltration is often a positive sign of muscle quality, this value is slightly outside the typical range. This is generally not a cause for concern but reflects your specific muscle composition.

textInfo.title: 17 mm Spleen Lesion, Consistent with a Benign Cyst
textInfo.medicalDescription: T2 stark hyperintense Läsion am Milz Unterpol (17 mm). Milzläsion am Unterpol vereinbar mit Milzzyste.
textInfo.originalDescription: T2 stark hyperintense Läsion am Milz Unterpol (17 mm)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI shows a 17 mm lesion at the lower tip of your spleen. This spot is described as 'T2 stark hyperintense,' which means it showed up very brightly on a specific MRI image type that is sensitive to water content. Based on this appearance, the finding is consistent with a splenic cyst, which is a benign, fluid-filled sac within the spleen.

textInfo.title: Low-Normal Folic Acid
textInfo.medicalDescription: Folsäure Serum
textInfo.originalDescription: Folsäure Serum ': L 11.2 nmol/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your folic acid (folate) level is 11.2 nmol/L, which is just below the lower limit of the normal range (>12.2 nmol/L) and falls into what the lab calls a 'grey area' (7.6-12.2 nmol/l). Folate is a B-vitamin important for cell growth and metabolism. Increasing intake of folate-rich foods like leafy greens and legumes may be beneficial.

textInfo.title: Family History: Father's High Blood Pressure
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient's father had a history of hypertension.
textInfo.originalDescription: Father: Used to suffer from high blood pressure.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Father

textInfo.title: Elevated Non-HDL Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: Non-HDL-Cholesterin
textInfo.originalDescription: Non-HDL-Cholesterin (berechnet): H 4.3 mmol/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your Non-HDL cholesterol level is 4.3 mmol/L, which is higher than the recommended level of less than 3.9 mmol/L. Non-HDL cholesterol includes all the 'bad' types of cholesterol and is a strong predictor of cardiovascular risk. Management often involves lifestyle changes and sometimes medication.

textInfo.title: No History of Elbow Injury
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient denies any history of trauma or injury to the elbows.
textInfo.originalDescription: I have never had any sort of injury on the elbows.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: You have never injured your elbows.

textInfo.title: Acidic Feeling in Throat/Mouth
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports an intermittent acidic sensation in the throat/mouth, occurring up to a few times a week.
textInfo.originalDescription: I also feel an acidic feeling in my throat/mouth. This happens sometimes up to a few times a week and sometimes less frequently.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: You sometimes feel an acidic sensation in your throat or mouth. This can happen up to a few times a week, but sometimes less often.

textInfo.title: Constant Left Achilles Tendon Inflammation
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports constant inflammation at the Achilles tendon insertion on the calcaneus since 2018. The pain is exacerbated by pressure, making supine rest difficult and requiring pillows for leg elevation during sleep. Certain footwear also causes pain.
textInfo.originalDescription: Since 2018, I feel a constant inflammation exactly where the Achilles Tendon connects to the heel. Lying on my back and resting the leg on the heel is impossible for me as it causes pain and discomfort and I have to sleep with pillows beneath my leg every night. Certain types of shoes can cause pain and discomfort as well.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Since 2018, you've had constant inflammation where your left Achilles Tendon connects to your heel. It's impossible to rest your heel on a surface while lying on your back due to pain, so you have to sleep with pillows under your leg every night. Some shoes also cause pain.

textInfo.title: Low HDL Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: HDL-Cholesterin
textInfo.originalDescription: HDL-Cholesterin ': L 0.99 mmol/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your HDL ('good') cholesterol level is 0.99 mmol/L, which is below the recommended level of greater than 1.00 mmol/L. Higher levels of HDL cholesterol are protective against heart disease. Lifestyle factors such as regular exercise and a healthy diet can help increase HDL levels.

textInfo.title: Small 14 mm Liver Lesion, Likely Benign
textInfo.medicalDescription: T2 hyperintense, rundliche Läsion subkapsulär in Segment V (14 mm). DD Zyste, Hämangiom.
textInfo.originalDescription: T2 hyperintense, rundliche Läsion subkapsulär in Segment V (14 mm,
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI revealed a small, round lesion measuring 14 mm, located just under the capsule (the outer lining) of your liver in an area known as segment V. This lesion is described as 'T2 hyperintense,' meaning it appeared bright on a specific type of MRI sequence, which is typical for fluid-filled structures. The radiologist suggests this is most likely either a simple cyst (a benign, fluid-filled sac) or a hemangioma (a non-cancerous tangle of blood vessels), both of which are common and generally harmless findings.

textInfo.title: Family History: Father's Atypical Parkinsonism
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient's father has a condition classified as Atypical Parkinsonism, characterized by Parkinson's-like symptoms but lack of response to Levodopa therapy. He was examined at Hirslanden.
textInfo.originalDescription: Father: Has a condition that is currently classified as Atypical Parkinsons since it shows Parkinson's like symptoms but he doesn't respond to Parkinson's therapy i.e. Levodopa. He did the examination in Hirslanden.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Father

textInfo.title: Family History: Father's Heart Stent
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient's father had a stent placed in 2013 due to unstable angina pectoris.
textInfo.originalDescription: Father: Stent placed due to unstable angina pectoris in 2013.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Father

textInfo.title: Elevated Lipase Level
textInfo.medicalDescription: Lipase '
textInfo.originalDescription: Lipase ': H 56 U/I (<53)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your lipase level is 56 U/l, which is slightly above the normal range of less than 53 U/l. Lipase is an enzyme produced by the pancreas to help digest fats. Elevated levels can sometimes be a sign of pancreas inflammation or other issues, though minor elevations can be temporary and not clinically significant.

textInfo.title: Family History: Father's Kidney Conditions
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient's father underwent a nephrectomy in 2017 due to a benign tumor. He also has a history of nocturia and other kidney issues.
textInfo.originalDescription: Father: Nephrectomy in 2017 due to a benign tumor. My father has a history of nocturia as well, including other kidney issues and he also removed one of his kidneys a few years ago due to a benign tumor.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Father

textInfo.title: Left Buttock Twitching
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports occasional, involuntary myoclonus (twitching) in the left buttock, similar to the twitching experienced in the right hand.
textInfo.originalDescription: I also feel involuntary twitching in my left butt sometimes, similar to the one in my hand.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: You sometimes feel involuntary twitching in your left butt, similar to the twitching in your hand.

textInfo.title: Past Surgery: Tonsil Removal
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient underwent a tonsillectomy as a child.
textInfo.originalDescription: Tonsils removal as a kid.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your tonsils were removed when you were a kid.

textInfo.title: Elevated Total Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: Cholesterin '
textInfo.originalDescription: Cholesterin ': H 5.3 mmol/l (<5)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your total cholesterol level is 5.3 mmol/l, which is higher than the recommended level of less than 5 mmol/l. Cholesterol is a waxy substance in your blood, and high levels can contribute to the risk of cardiovascular disease.

textInfo.title: Right Wrist Grinding and Cracking (No Pain)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports significant crepitus in the right wrist upon circular motion, but denies associated pain.
textInfo.originalDescription: Moving my right hand in circles results in a lot of grinding and cracking of the wrist, but no pain.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: When you move your right hand in circles, you experience a lot of grinding and cracking sounds from your wrist, but it is not painful.

textInfo.title: Ankle Medical Workup and Treatment History
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient has undergone multiple physician consultations and diagnostic workups (MRI, X-ray) for left ankle symptoms. A finding of minor lateral instability was identified, but imaging was otherwise unremarkable. Conservative treatments (physiotherapy, osteopathy) have been ineffective.
textInfo.originalDescription: I have been to multiple doctors (balgrist, hirslanden, fusschirurgie-zuerich). Doctors were able to identify some minor lateral instability, however all MRIs and X-Rays came clean and no one wanted to proceed with further examination. Everyone just dismissed the case with Physiotherapy - I've done multiple rounds of physiotherapy and osteopathies through the years which didn't help at all.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: You've seen multiple doctors (Balgrist, Hirslanden, Fusschirurgie-Zuerich) for your ankle. They found some minor lateral instability, but all your MRIs and X-Rays were normal. No one recommended further investigation, and treatments like physiotherapy and osteopathy haven't helped over the years.

textInfo.title: Small Umbilical Hernia (11 mm)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Kleine fetthaltige Umbilikalhernie (11 mm, Serie 59, Bild 76).
textInfo.originalDescription: Kleine fetthaltige Umbilikalhernie (11 mm, Serie 59, Bild 76).
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The MRI of your abdomen identified a small umbilical hernia, measuring 11 mm. This means there is a small opening in the muscular wall of your abdomen at the navel (belly button), through which a small amount of fat is protruding. This is a common condition and, in this case, is noted as being small and containing only fat.

textInfo.title: Elevated Triglycerides
textInfo.medicalDescription: Triglyceride
textInfo.originalDescription: Triglyceride ': H 2.3 mmol/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your triglyceride level is 2.3 mmol/L, which is higher than the recommended level of less than 2.0 mmol/L. Triglycerides are a type of fat found in your blood, and high levels can increase the risk of heart disease. Lifestyle changes such as diet and exercise can help lower these levels.

textInfo.title: Elevated LDL Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: LDL-Cholesterin
textInfo.originalDescription: LDL-Cholesterin ': H 3.7 mmol/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your LDL ('bad') cholesterol level is 3.7 mmol/L, which is above the recommended level of less than 3.0 mmol/L. High LDL cholesterol contributes to plaque buildup in arteries, increasing the risk of heart attack and stroke. Lifestyle changes are the first step in lowering LDL levels.

textInfo.title: Elevated Urea
textInfo.medicalDescription: Harnstoff
textInfo.originalDescription: Harnstoff': H 8.8 mmol/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your urea level is 8.8 mmol/L, which is slightly above the normal range of 1.7-8.3 mmol/L. Urea is a waste product from protein metabolism that is filtered by the kidneys. Elevated levels can be related to kidney function, dehydration, or a high-protein diet.

textInfo.title: Worsening Hand & Finger Pain and Stiffness
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports onset of arthralgia and stiffness in hands and fingers around 2019/2020, with significant worsening since 2024. The pain is localized to the knuckles and finger joints. Since September 2025, there has been new, extremely high pain in the right index finger knuckle with specific movements. Stiffness is global but more pronounced in the right hand, impairing fine motor control and requiring massage for relief.
textInfo.originalDescription: My issues started around 2019/2020 with pain and stiffness in the hands and fingers. Since 2024, it has been a lot worse. The pain is mostly in the knuckles and the finger joints, and since September 2025 I also developed an extremely high pain in the knuckle of my right index finger on certain movements. The stiffness is felt everywhere and is especially noticeable in my right hand. I just feel like I cannot do fine motor control movements anymore and I have to constantly massage my hands to try and alleviate the symptoms.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: You began having pain and stiffness in your hands and fingers around 2019/2020, which has gotten much worse since 2024. The pain is mainly in your knuckles and finger joints. Since September 2025, you've also had very sharp pain in the knuckle of your right index finger with certain movements. The stiffness is everywhere but is worse in your right hand, making it hard to do fine movements and forcing you to constantly massage your hands.

textInfo.title: Low Corrected Calcium Level
textInfo.medicalDescription: Albumin-korrigiertes Calcium
textInfo.originalDescription: Albumin-korrigiertes Calcium: L 2.09 mmol/l (2.10-2.60)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your albumin-corrected calcium level is 2.09 mmol/l, which is slightly below the normal range of 2.10-2.60 mmol/l. This measurement adjusts for the amount of calcium bound to the protein albumin, giving a more accurate picture of 'active' calcium in your blood. Calcium is vital for bone health, muscle function, and nerve signaling.

textInfo.title: Nighttime Finger Numbness
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports occasional nocturnal paresthesia affecting the ulnar nerve distribution (pinky and ring finger), which has become rare since adopting a supine sleeping position.
textInfo.originalDescription: Sometimes I wake up in the middle of the night with my pinky and ring finger fully numb, but this rarely happens since I started sleeping on my back.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: You sometimes wake up in the middle of the night with your pinky and ring finger completely numb, though this happens much less often since you started sleeping on your back.

textInfo.title: History of Severe Right Forearm Fracture
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient has a history of a double fracture with significant dislocation of the distal radius in 2007.
textInfo.originalDescription: I've also had a double fracture with a big dislocation of the Radius bone on the forearm, right above the wrist in 2007.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: You had a severe injury in 2007, involving a double fracture and a major dislocation of the radius bone in your right forearm, just above the wrist.

textInfo.title: hs-CRP Indicates Intermediate Cardiovascular Risk
textInfo.medicalDescription: CRP sensitiv
textInfo.originalDescription: CRP sensitiv ': 2.4 mg/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your high-sensitivity C-Reactive Protein (hs-CRP) level is 2.4 mg/L. This value falls within the 1.0 - 3.0 mg/L range, which is associated with an intermediate risk of future cardiovascular events. hs-CRP is a marker of inflammation in the body.

textInfo.title: Elevated LDL Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: LDL-Cholesterin '
textInfo.originalDescription: LDL-Cholesterin ': H 3.7 mmol/l (<3.0)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your LDL (Low-Density Lipoprotein) cholesterol level is 3.7 mmol/l, which is above the recommended level of less than 3.0 mmol/l. LDL is often called 'bad' cholesterol because high levels can lead to a buildup of plaque in your arteries, increasing your risk for heart disease.

textInfo.title: Elevated Lipase
textInfo.medicalDescription: Lipase
textInfo.originalDescription: Lipase: H 56 U/I
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your lipase level is 56 U/l, which is slightly above the recommended level of less than 53 U/l. Lipase is an enzyme produced by the pancreas to help digest fats. Mildly elevated levels can have many causes and are often not significant, but it's a marker that might be monitored.

textInfo.title: Bright Spot in Brain Vein, Likely Technical Artifact
textInfo.medicalDescription: Hyperintensität am Sinus sigmoideus rechts, a.e. Flussartefakt entsprechend (Serie 10). Ansonsten regelrecht.
textInfo.originalDescription: Hyperintensität am Sinus sigmoideus rechts, a.e. Flussartefakt entsprechend (Serie 10). Ansonsten regelrecht.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your brain scan shows a bright spot on the right sigmoid sinus, a large vein that drains blood from the brain. The radiologist believes this is most likely a 'flow artifact,' which is a technical effect of the MRI machine capturing the movement of blood, not a true abnormality in the vein itself. The rest of the blood vessels in your brain appeared normal.

textInfo.title: Family History: Father's Gallbladder Removal
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient's father underwent a cholecystectomy in 2014.
textInfo.originalDescription: Father: Cholecystectomy in 2014.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Father

textInfo.title: Elevated Urea Level
textInfo.medicalDescription: Harnstoff'
textInfo.originalDescription: Harnstoff': H 8.8 mmol/l (1.7-8.3)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your urea level is 8.8 mmol/l, which is slightly above the normal range of 1.7-8.3 mmol/l. Urea is a waste product formed in the liver and removed by the kidneys. Elevated levels can sometimes indicate issues with kidney function or dehydration.
